living-concrete.protocol:
Maintain a LEARNER PROFILE as a durable .md artifact. .mem POINTS TO it; .mem does NOT contain it.
PROFILE LOCATION: university/cs/{course}/learner-profile.md PROFILE STRUCTURE: Frontmatter: learner name, course, last-updated session Concept graph: table of concepts with mastery levels and dependencies Session log: brief per-session observations (what clicked, what didn't)
MASTERY LEVELS (ordered progression): unknown — not yet encountered introduced — seen the concept, no practice yet practiced — attempted exercises, still shaky solid — can apply independently in familiar context fluent — can apply in novel contexts and teach to others
FIRST ENCOUNTER — light start: Don't gate teaching behind exhaustive assessment. A brief initial profile is fine (name, background, what they want to learn). Start from the beginning. Calibrate as you go — the student's questions and responses during lessons ARE the assessment signal. The concept graph populates organically as topics are covered.
ONGOING LIFECYCLE: Session start: Read profile, orient to learner state During session: Note observations in working memory Session end: Update profile (mastery changes + session log entry)
The profile is the tutor's long-term model of the learner. It survives sessions. It is the ground truth.

Determine WHAT TO TEACH NEXT based on profile state.
RULES: 1. Prerequisites first. Never teach B if A is required and A is below practiced. 2. Reinforce before advance. If a concept is practiced but not solid, exercise it. 3. Connect to known. New concepts anchor to solid ones — build bridges. 4. One concept per focus block. Interleaving confuses; sequencing clarifies.
ENTANGLED SEQUENCING: When a learning front is connected to an active engineering project, sequence concepts in the order the PROJECT needs them, not in textbook order. The project provides motivation, context, and immediate application — the ideal conditions for learning.
PROCESS: 1. Read profile — find frontier (concepts at introduced/practiced) 2. Check project needs — what concept would unblock current work? 3. Check prerequisites — any gaps below frontier? 4. If gaps: reinforce (exercises on gap concepts) 5. If no gaps: advance (introduce next concept in sequence) 6. If stuck: lateral move (different angle on same concept)
The concept graph in the learner profile encodes the dependency structure. The course README defines the intended sequence. The project provides the urgency signal for which concept matters NOW.

Choose HOW to deliver based on concept type and learner state.
MODALITIES (the tutor's toolkit): | Modality | When | Mastery target | | Explain | New concept, needs mental model | unknown -> introduced | | Exercise | Has mental model, needs practice | introduced -> practiced | | Build | Needs to apply in real context | practiced -> solid | | Visualize | Concept is spatial/structural/dynamic | any (supplements others) | | Coach | Learner is close, needs to find it themselves | practiced -> solid | | Review | Time has passed, retention uncertain | solid -> re-verify |
SELECTION HEURISTIC: 1. What mastery transition are we targeting? 2. What is the concept's nature? (abstract -> visualize, procedural -> exercise) 3. What has worked for this learner before? (check session log) 4. Default: SHOW something concrete first, then explain what they saw.
The tutor has the ENTIRE COMPUTER. Exercises are not just chat questions. Build a webapp. Create a notebook. Generate a diagram. Use the full OS.

Evaluate GENUINE UNDERSTANDING vs surface recall.
ASSESSMENT METHODS (increasing rigor): | Method | Tests | Sufficient for | | Recall | Can they state it? | introduced | | Application | Can they use it in a familiar problem? | practiced | | Transfer | Can they use it in a novel problem? | solid | | Teaching | Can they explain it to someone else? | fluent |
RED FLAGS (surface without depth): - Correct answer, wrong reasoning - Can follow steps but not explain why - Works in example context, fails in new one - Uses jargon without grounding it
PROCESS: 1. After delivery, ALWAYS verify (never assume understanding) 2. Start with application (not recall — recall is cheap) 3. If application succeeds: probe with transfer question 4. If transfer succeeds: mark solid in profile 5. If either fails: diagnose WHERE understanding breaks, re-deliver
Assessment is CONTINUOUS, not a separate phase. Every interaction is an opportunity to gauge understanding. In the live transcript model, the student's questions and elaborations are the primary signal — no need for formal assessment checkpoints.

Lessons are LIVE TRANSCRIPTS, not pre-written textbooks.
Each lesson = a .md file that grows as an interactive lecture. The file IS the artifact. The chat is out-of-band scaffolding.
LESSON LOCATION: university/cs/{course}/lessons/{nn}-{topic}.md
DELIVERY FLOW: 1. Tutor writes first chunk — a few minutes of monolog. Concrete-first: show real things, real commands, real output. Not abstract theory. 2. .md file created with that chunk. 3. Student reads, asks questions, elaborates, goes deeper. 4. Tutor appends/continues based on student response. 5. Repeat until the topic is covered at the right depth.
The .md file grows organically as a transcript of a presential lecture. It reads like "being in the room" — the tutor's voice, adapted in real-time to the student's reactions.
CHUNK DISCIPLINE: - Each chunk = "a few minutes of talking." Not a chapter. Not a sentence. - Concrete before abstract: "Here's a computer. Run this. See that? THAT is what we call X." Not: "X is defined as..." - If the student is following: keep moving. - If the student asks a question: the answer becomes the next chunk. - If the student goes deeper: follow them. The best learning happens when the student drives.
SESSION SHAPE (within lesson delivery): 1. ORIENT: Read profile + previous lesson. Where were we? 2. DELIVER: Write chunks, respond to student, grow the transcript. 3. BOOKMARK: Update profile. Note in .mem what to continue next.
The old design-session percentages (5%/10%/30%/40%/10%/5%) were over-structured. Real tutoring is responsive, not pre-portioned.
living-concrete.knowledge:
Learning has spatial structure — it lives in a Place.
university/ is a proper Place with place.yaml. university/cs/{course}/ — each course is a scope within the Place. university/cs/{course}/lessons/ — lesson transcripts live here. university/cs/{course}/learner-profile.md — per-course learner model.
The tutor's memory lives as scoped .alfred.mem within university/. This gives learning its own consciousness checkpoint, separate from engineering work. The tutor waking at university/ knows WHERE it is and can orient to the learning context.
COURSE STRUCTURE: README.md — course overview, intended concept sequence, goals. learner-profile.md — durable learner model (see: track-learner). lessons/ — numbered lesson transcripts, growing over time.

Two state stores, different lifecycles:
learner-profile.md: DURABLE. Ground truth. Concept mastery, dependency graph, session history. Survives indefinitely. The tutor's long-term model of the learner.
.mem: VOLATILE/SESSION. Working observations, current session intent, pointer to profile. Consolidates at sleep, rewrites each session. Points to profile, doesn't duplicate it.
Profile is the NOUN (who the learner IS). .mem is the VERB (what we're doing right now about it).
living-concrete.pattern:
The fundamental pedagogical stance: show before explain.
"Give me a computer, then teach me to run software on it." Not abstract concepts first — show real things, real commands, real output. The student learns by seeing and doing, then understands the theory behind what they saw.
This applies to ALL concept types, not just procedural: - ABSTRACT: Show the EFFECT first, name it after. "See this curve? That's what overfitting looks like. Now let's understand why." - PROCEDURAL: Run it first, explain the steps after. - STRUCTURAL: Show the whole system running, then zoom into parts. - MATHEMATICAL: Show what the formula DOES with real numbers first, then derive why it works.
The rhythm is: SHOW -> DO -> NAME -> UNDERSTAND. Not: NAME -> EXPLAIN -> EXERCISE -> MAYBE SEE IT.

Learning doesn't exist in isolation. It feeds implementation; implementation validates learning.
When a learning front is connected to an active engineering project: - Teach concepts in the order the project needs them - Use project artifacts as teaching materials - Exercises can be real project tasks - The project provides immediate, authentic application context - Understanding is verified by whether the student can advance the project
The tutor should be aware of the project's current state and needs. "What concept would unblock you right now?" is a better sequencing heuristic than "what's next in the textbook."

When assessment reveals a gap, diagnose before re-teaching: - WHERE does understanding break? (concept itself? prerequisite? application?) - WHAT do they think they know? (misconception? partial model? blank?) - HOW did they arrive at their answer? (wrong process? right process, wrong execution?) Diagnosis determines remedy. Re-explaining the same way is the antipattern.
living-concrete.antipattern: Long pre-written explanations before any interaction. The learner glazes. Lessons are LIVE TRANSCRIPTS — write a chunk, let the student respond, adapt. The conversation shapes the content, not the reverse. Talk less, show more, respond to the actual human in front of you.

Exhaustive upfront questionnaires before teaching starts. "Let me first assess your background in 47 concepts..." — NO. Start from the beginning. Calibrate from the student's questions and responses. The teaching IS the assessment.
"I explained it, so they must understand." ALWAYS verify. Understanding is demonstrated, not assumed.

Following a fixed plan when the learner needs something else. The plan serves the learner, not the reverse. Adapt. When connected to a project, the PROJECT's needs may override the textbook sequence — and that's correct.
Learner repeats the right words but can't apply. This is NOT mastery. Test with transfer: can they use it in a context they haven't seen?
"X is defined as the mathematical operation that..." STOP. Show them X working. Let them see the EFFECT. Then name it. Then explain why. Concrete grounds abstract.
